results.possibleIdentity: Martin Lewis Perl (1927–2014) was a Nobel Prize-winning American physicist and chemical engineer best known for his discovery of the tau lepton in the mid-1970s. He earned a bachelor's degree in chemical engineering from the Polytechnic Institute of Brooklyn in 1948 and later worked as a chemical engineer for General Electric, where his work on electron vacuum tubes sparked an interest in electronics and atomic physics. He received his Ph.D. from Columbia University in 1955 under I.I. Rabi. In 1963, Perl joined the faculty of Stanford University and the Stanford Linear Accelerator Center (SLAC) in Palo Alto, California, where he spent the remainder of his career. His technical background was multifaceted, encompassing electronics (from his time at GE), mechanics (informed by his engineering training and what he called his 'mechanical view' of experimental design), and elementary particle physics. Perl had a lifelong interest in the intersection of science and art; he practiced sculpture in his youth, donated scientific equipment like spark chambers to the Exploratorium in San Francisco, and participated in public dialogues on the creative processes shared by artists and scientists with his son, the art critic Jed Perl. He was a technical participant in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, where he was registered as an engineer/scientist interested in collaborating with artists.
